mcqs.question: In a recoverable schedule, a transaction T2 that reads data written by T1 should:

options.text: Wait until T1 commits or aborts
Write to the database before T1 commits

mcqs.correctAnswer: Wait until T1 commits or aborts
mcqs.explanation: In a recoverable schedule, a transaction must wait until the transaction that wrote the data either commits or aborts before it can commit itself.

mcqs.question: Which type of schedule provides the highest level of recoverability?
mcqs.explanation: Strict schedules provide the highest level of recoverability as transactions can only read/write data items after all uncommitted writers have completed.

mcqs.question: A schedule where no transaction reads data written by an uncommitted transaction is called:
mcqs.explanation: Cascadeless schedules ensure that a transaction only reads values written by committed transactions, preventing cascading aborts.

mcqs.question: If T1 writes X and T2 reads X, then T2 commits before T1 commits. This schedule is:
mcqs.explanation: This schedule is not recoverable because T2 committed after reading uncommitted data from T1. If T1 aborts, T2 cannot rollback properly.

mcqs.question: Every cascadeless schedule is:
mcqs.explanation: The hierarchy is: Strict → Cascadeless → Recoverable. Every cascadeless schedule is recoverable but not necessarily strict.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a conflict operation?

options.text: Read by T1, Write by T2 on same data
Write by T1, Write by T2 on same data
Read by T1, Read by T2 on same data
Write by T1, Read by T2 on same data

mcqs.correctAnswer: Read by T1, Read by T2 on same data
mcqs.explanation: Read-read operations are non-conflicting because they don't modify data. All other combinations involving at least one write are conflicts.

mcqs.question: A serial schedule is always:
mcqs.explanation: A serial schedule is always recoverable and conflict-serializable because transactions execute one after another with no interleaving.

mcqs.question: Cascading rollback occurs in:
mcqs.explanation: Cascading rollback happens when one transaction aborts and causes other transactions that read its uncommitted data to also abort.

mcqs.question: Two schedules are conflict equivalent if:
mcqs.explanation: Conflict equivalence requires that both schedules have the same set of operations and the order of all conflicting operations is the same.

mcqs.question: If a schedule is conflict serializable, it is always:
mcqs.explanation: Every conflict serializable schedule is also recoverable. However, the converse is not true - recoverable schedules may not be conflict serializable.

mcqs.question: The precedence graph (or serializability graph) is used to test:
mcqs.explanation: Precedence graph is a directed graph used to test conflict serializability. If the graph has no cycles, the schedule is conflict serializable.

mcqs.question: A schedule that can be transformed into a serial schedule by swapping non-conflicting operations is:
mcqs.explanation: Conflict serializable schedules can be transformed into a serial schedule by swapping only non-conflicting operations.

mcqs.question: Which problem occurs due to uncommitted writes being read?
mcqs.explanation: Dirty read happens when a transaction reads data that was written by another uncommitted transaction, which may be rolled back later.

mcqs.question: In strict schedules, a transaction can read/write a data item only after:
mcqs.explanation: Strict schedules require that any read or write of a data item happens only after all transactions that previously wrote that item have committed or aborted.

mcqs.question: If T1: W(X), T2: R(X), T1: Commit, T2: Commit - This schedule is:
mcqs.explanation: This is cascadeless because T2 reads only after T1 commits. It is also recoverable and strict.

mcqs.question: A schedule containing blind writes is:

options.text: May be view serializable but not conflict serializable

mcqs.correctAnswer: May be view serializable but not conflict serializable
mcqs.explanation: Blind writes (writes without reading) may make a schedule view serializable but not conflict serializable. Testing view serializability is NP-complete.

mcqs.question: The number of vertices in a precedence graph equals:
mcqs.explanation: A precedence graph has one vertex for each transaction in the schedule. Edges represent conflicting operations.

mcqs.question: A schedule is recoverable but not conflict serializable - such schedules:

options.text: Exist and are avoided in practice

mcqs.correctAnswer: Exist and are avoided in practice
mcqs.explanation: Such schedules exist but are avoided in practice because they may produce inconsistent results while appearing recoverable.

mcqs.question: T1: W(X), T2: W(X), T1: Commit - This schedule (partial) is:
mcqs.explanation: We cannot determine recoverability without knowing when T2 commits or aborts and what other operations exist in the schedule.

mcqs.question: For a schedule to be recoverable, the commit operation of a transaction should occur:

options.text: After all transactions whose data it reads have committed

mcqs.correctAnswer: After all transactions whose data it reads have committed
mcqs.explanation: In a recoverable schedule, a transaction can only commit after all transactions whose uncommitted data it has read have either committed or aborted.
